user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  Total assets have oscillated in a narrow band over the last 13 weeks, with no clean directional trend. The series shows week-to-week noise of $10-15B (e.g., 4501695 to 4488840 to 4482349 in the last three prints) rather than a smooth decline consistent with steady QT runoff at a fixed monthly cap. The 4-week change (-4238) and 13-week change (-6990) are both modestly negative, and the 52-week change (-17715) confirms a mild net drain over the past year, but the pace is very slow relative to earlier QT episodes and the level sits close to its 13-week average (4487401.62), just ~0.5% below the 52-week high. This looks like a balance sheet in slow, bumpy runoff rather than either renewed expansion or aggressive shrinkage — consistent with a low monthly cap or partial reinvestment offsetting maturities. Without the FOMC statement text I can't confirm whether caps were recently lowered or reinvestment policy changed, but the pure measurement trend points to continued modest net decline. I'll call this a lean toward down/runoff, but with low-to-moderate conviction given the noisy, non-monotonic path and the small magnitude of change relative to typical weekly volatility.
Would change its mind if: A clear rebound above 4495000 sustained over 2-3 weeks, or FOMC language announcing halt/reversal of runoff, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_13w_avg

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, persistent uptrend: from -0.47 thirteen weeks ago to -0.28 now, with the 4-week change (+0.07) and 13-week change (+0.17) both positive and the latest print sitting at the 52-week high (range_position = 1.00). Momentum has been steady and monotonic across nearly all 13 observations—no reversals, no plateaus—indicating conditions are tightening from an unusually loose base. Despite this trend, the index remains negative (-0.28), meaning conditions are still looser than the historical average, so this is tightening off a very accommodative starting point rather than a shift into restrictive territory. The consistency of the move (every single observation step is flat-to-up, mostly up) gives high confidence that the trend continues rather than mean-reverts abruptly within a single month. There is no sign of deceleration in the most recent prints (-0.31→-0.30→-0.28), if anything the pace is holding or slightly accelerating over the last few weeks. Absent a shock, I expect the index to continue rising modestly toward month end, extending the tightening trend, though it will likely still remain below zero.
Would change its mind if: A reversal or flattening in the weekly series (e.g., two consecutive declines) or 4w change turning negative would signal the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y breakeven has recovered from the 1.28 trough to 1.41 over the last few prints, with the last three readings (1.36→1.40→1.42→1.41) showing a modest but consistent uptrend after the dip. Despite this near-term firming, the longer-window changes (63d -0.07pp, 252d -0.20pp) remain negative, and the series still sits deep in the lower fifth of its 12-month range (range_position 0.20), well below the 1.94 high and 0.59pp under the 2% objective. This is a mixed picture: short-term momentum has turned modestly positive, but the level remains historically depressed and the medium/long trend is still down. Given the last four data points show higher-lows and higher-highs off the trough, I lean toward continued stabilization/modest drift up rather than renewed decline, but the pullback from 1.42 to 1.41 in the very last observation tempers conviction. There isn't enough strength in the recent bounce to call a strong reversal — it looks more like consolidation just off the 12-month low than a decisive re-acceleration toward target. I'll call a slight upward lean given the multi-week higher-low pattern, but with low-moderate conviction given the still-negative medium-term trend and the tiny pullback in the latest print.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.35 over the next few prints, or renewed negative 21d momentum, would confirm the medium-term downtrend is intact and invalidate the up call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Headline CPI y/y has surged from near-zero readings a year ago to 1.24% in the latest print, a sharp acceleration in year-on-year terms (yoy_range_position at 1.00 shows we're at the top of the 12-month range). However, the underlying momentum tells a different story: headline_mom has been flat-to-negative in the last three prints (0.12, -0.11, -0.05), and headline_3m_annualized is actually negative (-0.14%). This means the big y/y jump was driven by base effects (weak prints rolling out of the comparison window) rather than fresh price pressure. Core PCE remains remarkably stable and below target, sitting at 1.19% y/y with a 3m annualized pace of only 0.83% — core_distance_from_target is -0.81pp, and core_yoy_change_3m is essentially flat (-0.04pp). Core is the better read on persistence, and it shows no acceleration at all.
  
  Given the outgoing month-over-month print being replaced next release, and current mom_gap_vs_outgoing of -0.26pp suggesting deceleration in sequential terms, the y/y print is likely to keep climbing somewhat further next month only if base effects persist, but the flat-to-negative recent mom trend caps upside. I lean toward the headline y/y modestly rising further due to base-effect math, but with low-to-moderate conviction since underlying momentum has stalled.
Would change its mind if: A sharp downside surprise in next month's headline mom print, or a large upward base-effect reversal, would flip this; watch whether mom stays near zero.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has fallen steadily over the trailing year, from 5.70% to 4.80%, with the most recent print being the 12-month low itself (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00, range_position = 0.00). The 3m, 6m, and 12m changes are all negative (-0.20, -0.40, -0.90 pp), showing consistent tightening momentum with no sign of reversal. The Sahm-style gap is essentially nil (0.17 pp, and mechanically zero given the low is the latest print), so there is no loosening signal whatsoever. The level is now 0.80 pp below the assumed natural rate of ~4%, actually meaning it's tighter than a 4% anchor would suggest — wait, distance_from_natural of +0.80 with rate at 4.80% and natural near 4% confirms the market is already tighter than normal, not looser. Momentum has been persistently downward for a full year with no single monthly uptick reversing course. Absent any evidence of stalling, the most probable next print continues this grind lower or holds flat given it's already at cycle lows. I lean toward continued tightness (unemployment flat-to-down), not a reversal upward, since there's no evidence of a turn.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing unemployment rising back toward 5.0-5.1% would signal the tightening trend has reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has drifted down to 1.94%, the lowest print in this series, with a sharp -0.38pp move over 21 days and continued (if smaller) softness over 63 days, even though the 252-day change remains positive. Range position at 0.32 shows the yield sitting in the lower third of its 12-month band (1.68-2.50), closer to the low than the high, consistent with a term premium that has been compressing rather than expanding recently. The 10s30s slope at 0.81pp is fairly wide, which could reflect long-end curve steepening pressure from duration supply, but the outright level move dominates: five of the last six readings have declined or held near 2.00-2.03 before the latest drop to 1.94. There's no policy language accompanying this data to explain the move (e.g., flight-to-quality vs. growth scare vs. shifting issuance expectations), which limits my ability to judge persistence. Absent that context, the momentum signal (short-term decline, low-in-range position) argues for continued softness or at least no reversal in the next month. I'll lean toward a modestly lower 10-year yield at month end, but conviction is capped given the lack of any qualitative driver information and the fact that the 252-day trend still points the other way.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above ~2.05-2.10% over the next couple weeks, or news of reduced issuance/safe-haven unwind, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  financial_conditions lacks risk_appetite — Would confirm whether tightening in NFCI is being driven by risk-off sentiment likely to persist or reverse.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI surprises often drive near-term breakeven moves and would clarify if the bounce has fundamental support.
  inflation_expectations lacks term_premium — Distinguishing nominal-yield-driven breakeven moves from genuine inflation expectation shifts would sharpen the call.
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Would clarify whether the recent headline yoy surge is feeding into forward expectations or seen as transitory/base-effect driven.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Issuance and QT pace directly drive long-end supply pressure and term premium direction.
  term_premium lacks risk_appetite — Flight-to-quality vs risk-on flows would explain whether the recent decline is safety-driven and likely to persist.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation shows fed_assets at 4,489,796, up from 4,483,629 the prior week, and the series has been oscillating in a tight band (roughly 4,482,000–4,502,000) for the past several months rather than showing the steady monthly declines characteristic of a decisive QT cap being run down. My prior call of "down" was too confident: the 4-week change is now +7,447M and the 13-week change is +12,708M, both positive, which is the opposite of what a runoff would produce. The 52-week change is also marginally positive (+3,071M), and assets are only ~15,000M below the 52-week high. This looks like a balance sheet that has stabilized near a plateau with noisy week-to-week fluctuations (likely reserve-management or bill-purchase operations) rather than clear expansion or contraction. Without policy language confirming a change in the runoff cap or reinvestment policy, I can't be confident this is a genuine shift to QE — it may just be short-term volatility around a flat trend. Given the recent positive 4w and 13w prints, I lean toward "flat," acknowledging the possibility of a mild uptick, but conviction is modest given the absence of any statement language and the still-choppy pattern.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 4483000 over 2-3 weeks would confirm renewed runoff; a clear push above 4500000 sustained would confirm expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up/tightening, 0.65) has largely played out through the 13-week window but has now stalled: after a steady climb from -0.44 to -0.27, the last three readings are -0.27, -0.27, -0.28 — flat then a tiny tick down. The 4-week change is exactly +0.00, and the most recent print is actually the lowest of the last four. This matches the falsifier I set previously (flattening/reversal), so I'm updating off the prior up view. Conditions remain the tightest of the past year (range_position 0.96, at the 52w high, still well below zero at -0.28 so easier than average historically) but the momentum that drove the tightening trend has clearly stopped. There's no fresh policy language shown to suggest renewed tightening pressure or a dovish pivot toward easing. With momentum flat and the level near a local ceiling, the most defensible call for the next month is flat, not a continuation of the up trend. Conviction is modest given the tiny reversal could just be noise, but the flattening is consistent enough across the last three points to lean away from further tightening.
Would change its mind if: A resumed rise (two consecutive weekly increases) or 4w change turning clearly positive would signal tightening has resumed; a sharp drop would signal easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has climbed steadily over the last several prints: 1.25→1.42, with the most recent six readings rising almost monotonically (1.31, 1.32, 1.36, 1.39, 1.41, 1.42). My prior "up" call is validated by this print — the series pushed well above the 1.35 level I flagged as the invalidation threshold, and 21d momentum, while flat over the most recent 21-day window (+0.00pp), reflects a plateau after a sharp run-up rather than a reversal, since all the gains are concentrated in the last few observations. The 63d and 252d changes remain negative, showing the medium-term trend down from a year ago, but the recent trajectory clearly shows a bottoming and reversal off the 12m low (1.18), with the range position now at 0.32 and rising. Breakevens remain 0.58pp below the 2% objective, so there's no overheating signal, but the direction of travel over the past month is unambiguously higher. Given the short-term stall in the very latest reading (1.41→1.42 is a small increment, and 21d change is flat), I'll temper conviction slightly versus a full-throated call, but the multi-week uptrend argument holds and I don't see evidence of renewed weakness.
Would change its mind if: A print back below ~1.38 or two consecutive negative 21d readings would signal the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 14 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.30) was wrong: headline yoy fell from 1.24 to 0.85, a sizeable drop, driven by a negative mom print (-0.13) that is now the third consecutive negative/near-zero mom reading (-0.11, -0.05, -0.13). This is a real deceleration in headline momentum, not just base effects: headline_3m_annualized has flipped negative (-1.14%), well below the outgoing mom pace, and mom_gap_vs_outgoing is -0.36pp. That said, core PCE — the better persistence read — actually ticked up this month to 1.41 from a tight 1.18-1.23 range, and core_3m_annualized (+1.40%) is running above the recent yoy print, suggesting underlying pressure has not collapsed and may even be firming slightly. The divergence is notable: headline is soft on falling mom, but core just posted its highest reading in the window. Given headline's own recent trend (three straight weak/negative mom prints) and the fact that yoy comparisons will face a favorable (low) base from last year's weak prints rolling out, I'd expect some mean-reversion upward in headline yoy next month unless mom stays deeply negative. Weighing the strong negative mom momentum against base-effect arithmetic, I lean toward another soft-to-flat print but with low confidence given the whipsaw and core's mixed signal.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline mom back toward +0.2-0.3% would signal reversal; continued negative mom would confirm further yoy softening.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 21 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.90% from 4.80%, a 0.10pp uptick that exactly matches the falsifier condition I set last time (a move back toward 5.0-5.1% would signal reversal). This is a modest but real bounce off the 12-month low, and it comes after the series had been grinding lower for months. The sahm_gap (+0.10pp) is small and well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine recessionary turn, so this is not yet a strong loosening signal. However, my prior call for further tightening (down) was wrong for this release - the rate rose, not fell. The 3m and 6m changes are still net negative (-0.20pp each) reflecting the earlier decline, but the most recent single-month move is up, and the series had already shown some flattening/chop in the middle (5.00, 5.10, 5.00 in the last few prints) suggesting the tightening trend may be losing momentum rather than continuing cleanly. Given the level is still low relative to the 12-month range (position 0.13, near the low end) but the immediate direction has turned, I lean toward a continued modest rise or flat reading next month rather than a resumption of the downtrend.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.80% (the 12-month low) would indicate the recent uptick was noise and tightening has resumed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_above_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted sideways-to-slightly-lower over the last ten readings (1.78→1.74), consistent with the prior "down" call playing out: 21d change -0.26pp, 63d -0.49pp, 252d -0.29pp all negative. The level sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, just 0.11pp above the 12m low of 1.63%), indicating the long end remains well-bid relative to its own history. The 30s10s slope is still steep at +0.87pp, suggesting the curve continues to price a normal upward-sloping long end but without acute long-bond selling pressure. There's no fresh evidence of a reversal above the 2.05-2.10% threshold I flagged previously, nor any sign of issuance/balance-sheet driven pressure lifting yields. The most recent print (1.74) is essentially flat versus the prior single reading (1.76) and within noise of the last few observations, so the sharp downward momentum seen in the 63d window appears to be stabilizing rather than continuing to accelerate. Given the yield is bouncing in a narrow 1.71-1.81 band over the last ten prints with no clear renewed downtrend or uptrend catalyst, I lean toward flat with a modest downward tilt, lower conviction than before since the fast deceleration phase seems to have passed.
Would change its mind if: A break above ~1.85-1.90% or below ~1.65% over the next few weeks would clarify direction; current range is too narrow to call confidently.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (4,492,857) is the highest in the recent window and caps a four-week rise of +3,061M, reversing the prior dip below 4,483,000. Looking at the trailing 13 weeks, assets are still net down slightly (-3,666M, -0.08%), consistent with the tail end of a QT-paced runoff, but the most recent month's move is clearly positive and the series has bounced off its local low (4,478,485) by roughly 14,000M over three readings. This looks like typical week-to-week noise around a flattening balance sheet rather than a resumption of aggressive runoff or a shift to expansion — the swings are small relative to the ~$4.49T level and don't show a sustained directional break. My prior flat call was reasonable given the choppy pattern, and the newest print doesn't invalidate it — if anything the last 3 readings' upward drift suggests the runoff pace has decelerated further or reserves add operations are offsetting redemptions, keeping the series essentially rangebound. I don't see a sustained break above 4,500,000 nor a sustained break below 4,483,000; the latest point is a bounce within a still-narrow band. I'll maintain flat, with a modest lean acknowledging the recent 4-week uptick.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break above 4,500,000 over 2-3 weeks would confirm expansion; a sustained break below 4,480,000 would confirm renewed runoff acceleration.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series troughed mid-window (-0.35 → -0.27) then reversed and has fallen for four consecutive readings (-0.27, -0.28, -0.30, -0.32, -0.34, -0.35), landing exactly at the series' most negative print. The 4-week change of -0.07 confirms clear renewed easing momentum, even though the 13-week change is still marginally positive (+0.02), reflecting the mid-period bounce that has since fully reversed. My prior flat call was wrong in direction of momentum: I was waiting for a sharp drop or clear negative 4w change to signal resumed easing, and that is exactly what has now materialized — a consistent multi-week decline back to the cycle low. Conditions remain well below zero (-0.35, distance from average), meaning financial conditions are looser than historical norms and are now loosening further, not stabilizing. With nfci_range_position having presumably fallen from the 0.71 given (that stat reflects the pre-reversal snapshot) back toward the low end of the 52-week range, the trend is unambiguous over the last month. Absent a policy or market shock reversing this, the momentum favors continued or at least sustained easing into month-end rather than a bounce back toward zero.
Would change its mind if: A reversal with two consecutive weekly increases or nfci_change_4w turning positive would indicate tightening has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (1.62) sits within a choppy range that has bounced between roughly 1.56 and 1.65 over the most recent stretch, after climbing from a 1.18 12-month low. My prior "up" call was directionally reasonable — the 21d (+0.14pp) and 63d (+0.09pp) changes are still positive — but the series has not made a clean new high; it peaked at 1.65 two observations ago and pulled back to 1.62, and the 252d change is still negative (-0.11pp), showing the longer trend has not fully reversed. Breakevens remain 0.38pp below the 2% objective, and range position (0.58 of the 12-month band) suggests we're in the upper-middle of the range, not breaking out. The recent path (1.57→1.65→1.62) looks more like consolidation/chop than a decisive move in either direction. Short-term momentum is mildly positive but has stalled at the top of a well-defined range twice now (1.63-1.65 area), and the pullback in the latest print tempers confidence in continued near-term upside. I'll moderate conviction from the prior call given the stall, while still leaning slightly up given positive short-term momentum and distance-from-target dynamics that have not deteriorated.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 1.65 with follow-through would confirm renewed uptrend; a drop below 1.56-1.57 would confirm range breakdown/reversal.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print vindicates the reversal I flagged as a possibility: headline mom rebounded to +0.31% (from -0.13%), pushing headline_cpi_yoy up slightly to 0.89% from 0.85%. That said, the yoy reading is roughly flat over the last two months (0.85→0.89) after a sharp run-up from -0.10 to 1.24 six prints back, so momentum has stalled at a high level relative to its own 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.74 — near the top of the recent band). Core PCE, which I weight more for persistence, has moved up two consecutive months (1.19→1.41→1.47), a genuine acceleration versus the flat ~1.2% plateau seen for most of the prior year. Core_3m_annualized at 1.67% is now running above the trailing yoy pace, suggesting further gradual upward drift in core is likely. With headline mom now positive and core clearly re-accelerating, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure on headline yoy next month, though the deceleration in mom_3m_avg (+0.04) urges some caution against a sharp move. Both core and headline are still well below the 2% target, so this is a modest re-acceleration off very low levels, not a return to problematic inflation.
Would change its mind if: A drop in headline mom back below zero or core_pce_yoy reversing its two-month climb would undercut this reacceleration call.

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print shows fed_assets at 8,757,460, down from the prior observation of 8,790,495 — a one-period decline of roughly $33B, and the series is now sitting at its 52w high minus $33B rather than at a fresh high. That said, the broader trend remains clearly expansionary: the 4-week change is +107,058, the 13-week change is +309,479 (+3.66%), and the 52-week change is +1,394,109. The level is comfortably above the 13-week average (8,636,952), meaning the recent uptrend has not been reversed — this looks like a single-observation pause or minor wobble rather than a shift to runoff. My prior "up" call was correct and vindicated by the 4w/13w figures printed since. Absent any policy language indicating a change in caps or reinvestment policy, I read this most recent dip as noise within an ongoing balance-sheet expansion. I'll maintain an "up" call but trim conviction slightly given the newest single data point ticked down, which introduces some uncertainty about whether the expansion pace is now flattening. Next month's reading will clarify whether this is a genuine inflection or just volatility around a still-rising trend.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-week decline in fed_assets below the 13-week average, or a statement announcing resumed/accelerated QT caps, would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high, assets_13w_avg

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call for continued tightening (up) is now weaker on the newest data. NFCI rose steadily from -0.65 to -0.54 over the 13-week window (13w change +0.11), consistent with the tightening trend I flagged. However, the last two prints show -0.54, -0.54, -0.55 — momentum has clearly stalled and the most recent tick actually reversed slightly. The 4-week change is now flat at +0.00, and the index sits at nfci_range_position 0.94, i.e. near the top of its 52-week range, which limits room for further easing-of-conditions reversal but also means the tightening impulse has largely played out for now. Level remains well below zero (-0.55), so conditions are still loose in absolute terms, but the question here is direction over the next month, not absolute level. Given the plateau and the tiny downtick in the last observation, I no longer see strong evidence for continued tightening; the series looks like it's consolidating near a local high. This is a case where my prior view is partially vindicated (tightening did occur) but the trend has now stalled, matching my own stated falsifier condition (deceleration in weekly increments). I'll shift to flat with modest conviction, acknowledging genuine uncertainty about whether this is a pause or a turn.
Would change its mind if: A clear move back above -0.54 (new high) would revive the tightening call; a drop back below -0.60 would signal renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.40) was wrong: breakeven_10y continued rising through the last several prints, touching 2.58 before a mild pullback to 2.56. The 21d (+0.12pp) and 63d (+0.19pp) changes confirm the uptrend has persisted, not reversed, and the 252d change (+0.60pp) shows a sustained year-long climb. The series sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range, well above the low and only slightly off the 12m high of 2.76, indicating market-implied inflation expectations remain firmly elevated relative to recent history. The single-tick dip from 2.58 to 2.56 is minor and within noise — one observation is not enough to call a reversal, especially against a backdrop of consistent multi-horizon gains. Distance from the 2% target (+0.56pp) is significant and has been widening, not narrowing. Given the momentum across all three lookback windows points the same direction, and the pullback is tiny relative to range width (0.77pp), I read this as a continuation or at most a pause in an uptrend rather than a turn. I am revising to a modest 'up' call, tempered because the last print did tick down and could presage stabilization near current levels.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.50 sustained over multiple readings, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal genuine reversal rather than pause.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.75
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again from 6.90 to 7.17, and the falsifier condition (soft m/m plus core stalling) did not materialize. Momentum remains clearly hot. Headline m/m printed 0.69%, well above the outgoing 0.24% and above the trailing 3-month average of 0.84% only slightly lower - still elevated. Headline 3m annualized is running at 10.5%, more than double the 3.86 pace implied by the trailing y/y, showing acceleration is intensifying, not fading. Core PCE y/y also continued climbing, 4.44 to 4.89, a 0.93pp increase over the past three prints - persistence is not just an energy/food story. I weight core here specifically because it confirms headline strength is broad-based rather than a commodity-driven spike, and both series are moving the same direction. Distance from the 2% target is now nearly 2.9pp on core, and headline sits at range-position 1.00 (a fresh 12-month high). With m/m prints still running near 0.7-0.9%, the arithmetic base effects favor another y/y increase next month unless m/m decelerates sharply. There is no sign of that deceleration in the data shown.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.30% alongside a stall or reversal in core PCE y/y would indicate momentum has broken and shift me toward flat/down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:

The unemployment rate is now at a fresh 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00, unrate_range_position = 0.00), sitting at the bottom of its 13-month range. The trend remains firmly downward: -0.80pp over 3 months, -2.00pp over 6 months, -2.80pp over 12 months — a rapid, sustained decline with no sign of deceleration in the most recent observations (5.10→4.70→4.50→4.10→3.90). The sahm_gap of +0.27pp is trivial and reflects the trailing low sitting essentially at the current print, not any loosening signal. The rate is now 0.10pp below the assumed natural rate of ~4%, indicating a historically tight labor market. Given the consistency and pace of decline across multiple horizons, momentum favors another tight print rather than a reversal. There's no evidence of stabilization — every recent data point printed a new low. I'll revise toward continued tightening (a further decline or at least a hold at this very low level), rather than expecting mean reversion upward with no supporting data.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 4.1-4.2% would confirm a stall/reversal; continued prints at or below 3.9% confirm ongoing tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.45) was not confirmed: yields did not decline further and instead stabilized near the recent high, printing 1.52 after touching 1.55, essentially flat over the last two readings. The 21d momentum (+0.09pp) is positive, not negative as would be needed to validate a "down" thesis, though 63d change is mildly negative (-0.03pp), showing a choppy, non-trending recent path. The level sits at 1.52%, in the 73rd percentile of its 12-month range (low 0.93, high 1.74), meaning the long end is still closer to the top of its range than the bottom — consistent with elevated term premium rather than a fresh flight-to-quality bid. The 10s30s slope at 0.38pp is below my 0.5pp re-widening threshold, so the falsifier for a firm "up" call isn't fully met either. Net: yields have gone sideways-to-slightly-up over the past month after a large 252d run-up (+0.59pp), stalling just under the recent 1.55 high with no clear rollover. Given the mixed but slightly firmer near-term momentum and lack of any renewed rally in the long end, I lean toward flat-to-modestly-up rather than sticking with the prior down call, which the data have not supported.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.45% with 21d and 63d momentum both turning clearly negative would revive the down case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

Given the confirmed break and the recent uptick clustering near the top of the short window, I lean toward a continued modest upward drift in term premium over the coming month, though conviction is limited by the still-narrow move and mixed medium-term signal.
